planTitle: Runtime Consolidation: Unified Bootstrap, Domain Validation, and Qualified-Role Utilities

content: Introduced `CosmonautsRuntime` (`lib/runtime.ts`) as a single immutable bootstrap container to eliminate registry divergence. Previously, `cli/main.ts`, `orchestration/index.ts`, and `agent-spawner.ts` each performed their own config+domain loading sequence independently, with `agent-spawner.ts` falling back to a static `createDefaultRegistry()` that imported 8 coding-domain agent files at module load time. Any consumer that forgot to pass a registry silently got the stale static one. The fix: one `CosmonautsRuntime.create()` call per process entry point, result shared across all consumers.

The dual-registry bug was triggered by optional `registry` parameters with `createDefaultRegistry()` as the default. `chain-runner.ts:resolveRegistry()`, `chain-parser.ts:parseChain(expression, registry?)`, and `agent-spawner.ts:createPiSpawner(registry?)` all silently fell back to a statically-built registry that reflected only the hardcoded coding-domain agents — not whatever domains were dynamically loaded at runtime. The symptom was agents resolving correctly in one code path but silently failing or using wrong definitions in another. Fix: remove all fallbacks and make `registry` required everywhere. Zero optional-registry parameters remain in the codebase.

All orchestration consumers must receive `AgentRegistry` (or a full `CosmonautsRuntime`) as an explicit dependency — never build one locally or fall back to a module-level default. Concretely: `createPiSpawner(registry: AgentRegistry, domainsDir: string)` — both required. `parseChain(expression, registry, domainContext?)` — registry required. `ChainConfig.registry` — required field. `AgentRegistry` constructor requires a `builtins: readonly AgentDefinition[]` parameter with no default. Any new consumer of agent definitions must accept the runtime or registry from its caller.

Use `lib/agents/qualified-role.ts` for all `<domain>/<agent>` string operations — never inline `indexOf('/')` or `split('/')`. The module exports four functions: `qualifyRole(id, domain?)` builds `'coding/worker'`; `unqualifyRole(qualified)` strips the domain prefix returning `'worker'`; `splitRole(qualified)` returns `{ domain: string | undefined, id: string }`; `roleToConfigKey(role)` maps `'coding/task-manager'` → `'taskManager'` for config lookup. `qualifyAgentId` in `runtime-identity.ts` is now a re-export alias of `qualifyRole` for backward compatibility. These replaced duplicated inline implementations in `agent-spawner.ts`, `chain-runner.ts`, `resolver.ts`, and `orchestration/index.ts`.

`CosmonautsRuntime` uses a static async `create()` factory (not a constructor) because domain loading requires dynamic `import()`. The returned object is `Object.freeze()`d — immutable after creation, no hot-reload. Domain changes during a session are not picked up; this is an accepted trade-off (domains don't change at runtime). `CosmonautsRuntimeOptions` requires `{ domainsDir: string; projectRoot: string; domainOverride?: string }`. `domainContext` resolves as `domainOverride ?? projectConfig.domain`.

`validateDomains()` in `lib/domains/validator.ts` is a standalone pure function independent of `CosmonautsRuntime`. This is deliberate: if validation were coupled to runtime construction, loader tests using minimal temp-dir fixtures would fail on missing prompts/capabilities. The validator is called inside `CosmonautsRuntime.create()` after `loadDomains()`, but tests can call `loadDomains()` directly and skip validation. Do not merge validation into the domain loader itself — keep the two steps separate.

The orchestration extension caches `CosmonautsRuntime` per-cwd using a `Map<string, Promise<CosmonautsRuntime>>` held inside the extension function closure — NOT at module level. Each extension instance gets its own cache map; this is what enables test isolation (tests that create fresh extension instances get fresh caches). A module-level cache would leak state across tests. The helper is `getRuntime(cwd: string): Promise<CosmonautsRuntime>` and is called by both `chain_run` and `spawn_agent` tool handlers.

When `cli/main.ts` migrated to receive `runtime.workflows`, `loadWorkflows`/`resolveWorkflow`/`listWorkflows` in `lib/workflows/loader.ts` rejected the value because those functions accepted `WorkflowDefinition[]` (mutable) while `runtime.workflows` is `readonly WorkflowDefinition[]`. Fix: widen all workflow loader function signatures to accept `readonly WorkflowDefinition[]`. Any future function that receives data from a frozen runtime object must accept `readonly` arrays — do not cast with `as WorkflowDefinition[]`.

Domain validation severity rules in `lib/domains/validator.ts`: **errors** (halt startup via `DomainValidationError`) for — missing persona prompt, unresolved capability, unresolved extension, unresolved domain lead. **Warnings** (logged to stderr, startup continues) for — subagent entry that doesn't resolve (may be a future domain), workflow stage that doesn't resolve to a known agent. Rationale: errors cover things that would cause immediate runtime failures; warnings cover forward-references that may be intentionally cross-domain. Do not promote subagent resolution to an error — agent definitions commonly reference agents in domains that haven't been loaded yet.

Migration order for consolidating scattered bootstrapping into `CosmonautsRuntime`: (1) `lib/agents/qualified-role.ts` + tests — pure utility, no deps, unblocks everything; (2) `lib/domains/validator.ts` + tests — depends only on domain types; (3) `lib/runtime.ts` + tests — composes config+domains+validation+registries; (4) `agent-spawner.ts` — remove defaults, import utilities; (5) `chain-runner.ts` + `chain-parser.ts` + `types.ts` — remove defaults, make registry required; (6) `cli/main.ts` + `cli/session.ts` — replace bootstrap block with runtime; (7) orchestration extension — replace `loadRuntimeDomainContext` with cached runtime; (8) `resolver.ts` + `agents/index.ts` cleanup — remove `createDefaultRegistry`, `resolveAgent`, static imports. This order avoids circular migration issues: utilities before consumers, runtime before CLI, CLI before final cleanup.
